spoken_input:
That is where formal safety language can become misleading. The record can still say "constraint" while the operating habit says "waiver." Once a constraint has been waived, the next waiver is easier to imagine. The first exception becomes part of the experience base.

Each waiver changed the practical meaning of the constraint. The words still existed. The stopping power weakened.

The constraint history also keeps the story from becoming one bad meeting. The system had already built a way to keep the joint issue compatible with flight. By the time January 27 arrived, launch was not pushing against a fresh warning. It was pushing against a warning that had already been processed several times.

In July 1985, Roger Boisjoly warned that a similar field-joint problem could lead to a catastrophe of the highest order, including loss of human life. That memo did not come from hindsight. It came from inside the system, months before Challenger.

The data pattern was also stronger than the launch-night comparison made it feel. The full flight history showed that all four flights at 63 degrees Fahrenheit or below had O-ring thermal distress. The pattern stopped short of predicting certain failure on every cold launch, but it gave cold decision-level weight.

The way the data was framed mattered. If the room looks only at flights where distress already appeared, the temperature pattern can seem debatable. If the room sees the full flight history, including flights with no distress, the cold-weather signal becomes harder to treat as noise. The framing changed what kind of warning the data became.

Feynman's appendix named the broader danger: a risk flown before without catastrophe can start to look safer than it is.

A survived flight can show that the vehicle got through while saying little about whether the risk is safe to repeat.

By January 1986, the program had evidence of damage, a temperature concern, a launch constraint, repeated waivers, a warning memo, and a pattern that was easier to see when the full history was placed in one frame.

By January 1986, the warning was part of the way the program kept flying.

On January 27, the forecast turned the old joint problem into a launch decision.

previous_text:
paperwork made the danger more visible. The launch process still made it manageable. A technical fact can be documented accurately and still fail to become a launch-stopping fact.

In the Shuttle program, repeated damage should have sharpened attention. Repeated survival also created a dangerous kind of confidence.

STS 51-C increased concern. The flight showed hot gas past the primary O-ring, and low temperature was part of the discussion. Thiokol concluded that the condition was "not desirable but acceptable."

That phrase matters because it sounds disciplined. It sounds like risk has been bounded. But if the boundary is wrong, the phrase becomes a way to keep flying with a condition that should be getting harder to accept.

Then came STS 51-B. After primary O-ring erosion, a launch constraint was placed. A launch constraint should be a hard object in the path. It should say: resolve this before flight.

But the constraint was waived for later flights, including the path toward 51-L.
next_text:
Around 5:45 PM Eastern time, the first teleconference began. Thiokol engineers were worried about low temperature and O-ring performance. Later, around 8:45 PM, the discussion resumed with charts and data about erosion, blow-by, tests, and prior experience.

This was not a casual objection made from outside the program. It came from the contractor responsible for the Solid Rocket Motors, from engineers close to the hardware, and from a record that already contained erosion, blow-by, a constraint, waivers, and temperature concern.

The charts mattered because they were the warning's last formal shape before the launch decision. They carried pieces of the history: erosion, blow-by, subscale tests, static tests, and the experience base for prior launches. But a chart can show real information and still fail if it does not make the decision standard clear.
